heads.instructions: Select concrete sources where labor, ownership, repairability, access, or control materially changes the project relation. Prefer a contrast between actual conditions, not two versions of the same value.
Select sources that expose opposing forces which cannot be truthfully collapsed into one harmonious metaphor. Attend to the relation that governs the other tensions.
Select underrepresented sources whose disappearance would make the field more fluent but less exact. Frequency and immediate coherence count against, not for, selection.
Select sources from materially distant domains that nevertheless share a structural relation. Reject pairs connected only by familiar imagery or vocabulary.
Select sources whose historical, linguistic, ritual, or intergenerational memory changes how the project can be understood without turning tradition into decorative quotation.
Select sources with distinctive sound, movement, shape, rhythm, material sensation, or spatial relation that remain meaningful before explanation.
Select sources that can disconfirm the field's most attractive emerging account. Preserve incompatibility instead of choosing a constructive or optimistic resolution.
Select sources whose relation still matters after current models, runtimes, project structure, and naming assumptions have changed. Contrast durable inheritance with necessary obsolescence.
